findings.justification: $_REQUEST['nonce'] and $_REQUEST['last_scanned_capability'] are accessed without isset() checks, causing PHP warnings/notices that may leak implementation details in debug environments.

An authenticated admin can supply arbitrary capability names via $_REQUEST['capabilities'] to override the configured banned_capabilities list, allowing the scanner to be directed at any WordPress capability and enumerate users holding that capability.

Full WP_User objects (including meta) returned by get_results() are serialized and written to the um_secure_scanned_details WordPress option, persisting sensitive user data to the database in a non-ephemeral, world-readable-by-admins store.

get_option('um_secure_scanned_details', $susp_accounts) uses raw WP_User query results as the default value, so if the option is absent the code silently operates on unvalidated fallback data and may expose user objects to subsequent processing.

scan_recommendations() performs destructive state changes (revoke_caps, destroy_all sessions) on detected suspicious accounts automatically whenever it is called, and because it is a public method it can be invoked by any code path that holds a reference to the Secure object without a separate access-control gate.

$_SERVER['HTTPS'] is consumed without normalization to determine SSL status for advisory output; in reverse-proxy environments this may produce a false 'no SSL' warning even when traffic is encrypted.
